title: Artifact viewing follow-ups from PR 7674 full review
author: azooz2003-bit
body:
Deferred items from CodeRabbit's full review of https://github.com/manaflow-ai/cmux/pull/7674, adjudicated as real but not merge-blocking. Each is scoped and independent.

- [ ] Tap hit-testing uses `String.count` for column extents, so wide (CJK) glyphs before a path skew the tappable range (TerminalArtifactTapHitTester.swift). Use cell widths.
- [ ] `AgentChatArtifactIndex` caches one snapshot per transcript identity with no eviction; a long-lived Mac session accumulates snapshots. Add LRU bound.
- [ ] `ChatArtifactFolderView` directory rows are inert; allow drilling into subdirectories (one-level scope rule still applies server-side).
- [ ] `ChatArtifactViewerSheet` progress ticks spawn unstructured Tasks that aren't cancelled on path change; a late tick from the previous file can briefly show wrong progress.
- [ ] `ChatBlockDetailSheetView` rows show View file while stat is loading/failed; represent loading/file/directory/failure explicitly.
- [ ] De-duplicate the chunked-fetch loops in `MobileChatEventSource.artifactFetch` / `terminalArtifactFetch`.
- [ ] `AgentChatSessionRegistry.currentOrMostRecentSessionRecord(surfaceID:)` scans all records per lookup; index by surface.
- [ ] Gallery first page stats every created/attached artifact unbounded; bound or paginate the stat work for pathological sessions.
- [ ] `ChatArtifactGalleryOrdering` re-sorts the full index per page request; cache the ordered snapshot per generation.
- [ ] `TerminalArtifactWire.payload(...) ?? [:]` turns encode failures into empty-success; surface an internal error instead.
- [ ] Chip count re-arm reuses the triggering request's localCount for the retried generation; have the surface own the freshest local count.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

title: Expose keyboard shortcut for surface/workspace reorder (reuse existing reorderSurface/reorderWorkspace)
author: yeoul9703
body:
### Problem or motivation

I'm always frustrated when I want to reorder a surface (tab) within a workspace, or reorder a workspace in the sidebar, and the only way to do it is mouse drag. cmux already has the backend logic for this — `Workspace.reorderSurface` and `TabManager.reorderWorkspace` power mouse-drag reorder, tmux mirror sync, and the `cmux reorder-surface` CLI/socket API — but there's no `ShortcutAction` to trigger it from the keyboard. TODO.md's "Add surface move/reorder commands" is checked for the agent/CLI side, but not for a human-facing keyboard binding.

### Proposed solution

Add new `ShortcutAction` cases (e.g. `moveSurfaceLeft`/`moveSurfaceRight`, `moveWorkspaceUp`/`moveWorkspaceDown`) that call the existing `reorderSurface`/`reorderWorkspace` functions with an adjacent-index target, following the same wiring pattern as `nextSurface`/`prevSurface` (`ShortcutAction.swift` → `ShortcutAction+Defaults.swift` → `KeyboardShortcutSettings.swift` → `Workspace+SurfaceNavigation.swift`), plus the corresponding `cmux.schema.json` / i18n message entries.

Related: #7734, #4503

title: Checklist popover viewport height assumes single-line rows
author: azooz2003-bit
body:
Review finding deferred from https://github.com/manaflow-ai/cmux/pull/7797 (Codex P2).

The checklist popover (and the inline sidebar checklist) sizes its scroll viewport from item COUNT with a single-line row-height estimate (`itemRowHeightEstimate * min(count, 6)`), but items wrap without a line limit. A checklist containing one long wrapped item gets a one-line-high viewport even though the row spans several lines, so the text is only reachable through a tiny clipped scroll area.

Fix direction: measure actual content height (bounded by the 6-row-equivalent cap) instead of deriving height purely from row count, or reintroduce a bounded line limit for the popover list.

title: iPad: polish sidebar header and terminal shortcut bar for marketing-quality UI
author: azooz2003-bit
body:
Two iPad UI issues surfaced while capturing App Store screenshots for https://github.com/manaflow-ai/cmux/pull/8037 (iPad was excluded from that set because of them).

**Sidebar header crowding (portrait split view).** The header row packs gear, Mac picker (monitor icon + "All Computers" + chevron), filter menu, new-workspace plus, and the sidebar toggle into one line. The monitor icon nearly touches the picker label and the filter icon overlaps the chevron, so the row reads as misaligned clutter.

**Terminal shortcut bar layout.** In the workspace detail, the accessory row (keyboard dismiss, record, compose, arrows, modifier keys, Tab, Esc, ^C, ^D, Claude/Codex/Ollama chips, arrow cluster) overflows: trailing arrow buttons clip at the right edge and spacing between chip groups is uneven, especially in portrait split view where the row spans both columns' width.

Repro: iPad Pro 13-inch (M5) simulator, iOS 26.5, portrait, dark mode, paired to a Mac with 8 workspaces. Screens: workspace split view and workspace detail.

Acceptance: header items align on one baseline with consistent gaps and no icon/label overlap; shortcut bar either scrolls cleanly with no half-clipped buttons or collapses groups to fit; both look intentional in portrait and landscape so iPad screenshots can join the App Store set.

title: Vault: browsable per-workspace agent session history (transcript, files touched, commits, resume) surviving restarts
author: austinywang
body:
## Problem

Users need to answer "what did that agent do yesterday and where did it leave off" and today cmux has no good answer. Session transcripts exist on disk and Vault indexes them, but there is no browsable, workspace-scoped history: once a session ends (or the app restarts), reconstructing what an agent actually did means manually spelunking JSONL transcripts, `git log`, and memory.

## Wanted

A browsable history of agent sessions per workspace, surviving app restarts, built on Vault (which already owns session indexing and is the natural place for this):

- **Timeline per workspace**: every agent session (Claude Code, Codex, and other Vault-indexed providers) with start/end time, provider, model, and status (completed / interrupted / still running).
- **Per-session detail view**:
  - transcript (rendered from the indexed session file, reusing Vault's existing readers)
  - files touched during the session
  - commits made during the session (correlate session time window + workspace clone; show short SHA + subject)
  - a **Resume** button that re-launches the agent with the right resume binding in the right workspace/surface
- **Durability**: history persists across app restarts and session exits; it is a record, not a view over live process state.

## Why now / pairing with auto-resume

This pairs directly with the auto-resume work (`terminal.autoResumeAgentSessions`, resume bindings): history is what makes resume trustworthy. A resume button next to a visible record of what the session did (and where it stopped) turns auto-resume from "hope it picks the right thing" into an informed action. It also gives resume a home when automatic binding fails (e.g. remote sessions, #7978's `resume_binding: null` case).

## Relationship to existing issues

- #6186 (first-class agent-session list across workspaces): complementary; that is a live thread-management surface, this is the durable per-workspace historical record. They should share the session index and likely UI components.
- #3322 (persist session manifest & re-attach after restart): the manifest work is a natural data source.
- #5617 (new session orphans prior thread's resume binding): history solves the "no way back" problem by keeping every prior session reachable.
- #7042 (Hermes sessions do not auto-resume), #4941 / #5822 (Vault discovery of remote/SSH sessions): the history view should render whatever Vault indexes, so provider/remote coverage improvements automatically appear here.
- #4531 / #4535: Vault indexer freshness and scan performance directly affect this view; bounded incremental indexing is a prerequisite for making history cheap to open.

## Sketch

- Data: extend the Vault session index with a per-workspace dimension (workspace id recorded at session registration; sessions launched outside a workspace grouped under their project/cwd). Persist enough metadata at session end (duration, exit reason, resume id, files-touched summary) that the list renders without opening transcripts.
- Files touched: derivable from tool-use records in transcripts at index time (cheap incremental pass), not by re-reading full transcripts on view.
- Commits: query the workspace clone's reflog/log for the session's time window at view time; no new state needed.
- UI: a History tab/section in the workspace context (sidebar or right panel), list -> detail -> resume. Reuse Vault's transcript preview components.
- Resume: reuse existing resume-binding machinery; where a binding is missing, offer "resume by session id" with the provider's native resume flag.

## Acceptance criteria

- From a workspace, one click shows all past agent sessions for that workspace, newest first, after an app restart.
- Opening a session shows transcript, files touched, and commits made during the session window.
- Resume from history launches the correct provider with the correct session id in that workspace.
- Opening history does not trigger unbounded transcript scans (bounded incremental index; cf. #4535).

title: Settings UI for BYO VPS onboarding (follow-up to #8003 Phase 2 CLI)
author: austinywang
body:
Phase 2 of #8003 landed one-command BYO VPS onboarding as CLI-first (`cmux vps add/list/status/upgrade/remove`) plus the workspace health/mode plumbing (`daemon_health` and `mode` in `workspace.remote.status`).

This issue tracks the deferred Settings UI entry point:

- A "VPS backends" section in Settings listing registered hosts from `~/.local/state/cmux/vps/hosts.json` with live health (running / degraded / needs-upgrade / unreachable), version drift, and session counts — driven by the same `VPSProvisioner.status()` signals as the CLI, not inferred connection state (#7828 class).
- Add/remove/upgrade actions that call through the same `CmuxVPSProvisioning` package paths as the CLI (shared behavior policy: one mutation path).
- Sidebar/inspector badge surfacing `WorkspaceRemoteDaemonHealth` and the "Direct (no cloud proxy)" mode label; the status payload already carries both.

All provisioning machinery (planner, executor, registry, health classification) is in `Packages/macOS/CmuxVPSProvisioning` and is UI-agnostic, so this is view + wiring work only.

title: Add a Git source-control view to the right sidebar (VS Code SCM-style: changes, commit, pull/push, commit graph)
author: austinywang
body:
### Problem or motivation

cmux has no built-in way to see and act on a workspace's git state. Reviewing what an agent changed, staging, committing, and syncing all require bouncing to a terminal or an external IDE. We already have right-sidebar tools (Files, Find, Vault, Sessions, Feed, Dock) and a standalone diff viewer (`cmux diff`), but no source-control surface that ties them together.

Add a Git view to the right sidebar, in the spirit of VS Code's Source Control panel, adapted to cmux's agent-centric workflows.

### Proposed solution

A new right-sidebar tool (its own icon in the vertical strip) showing the current workspace repo's state. Reference design: a competing product's git panel; the elements that matter:

- **Compare header**: current branch vs its upstream (e.g. `vs origin/main`) with ahead/behind counts and a jump-out affordance to open the branch/PR on GitHub.
- **Changes section**: staged/unstaged/untracked files with per-file status badges; clicking a file opens its diff in the existing `cmux diff` viewer; hover/context actions for stage, unstage, discard.
- **Commit flow**: message field + commit button (and amend), so quick commits do not require a terminal.
- **Sync actions**: prominent Pull/Push button with a dropdown for variants (pull rebase, fetch, force-push-with-lease), reflecting ahead/behind state. Empty state reads like the reference: "No changes on this branch" with the relevant sync action.
- **Commits section**: collapsible log with a mini graph of local commits vs incoming upstream changes ("Incoming Changes" group when behind), expandable per-commit file lists.
- Live updates via the existing git metadata infrastructure; must not regress typing-latency work from #4705 (no heavyweight polling; reuse the debounced gitIndexSnapshot pipeline).

Integration requirements:

- Registered like the other right-sidebar tools: reachable via `cmux right-sidebar` CLI (new `git` verb, consistent with #3808), a customizable keyboard shortcut via KeyboardShortcutSettings, and the action-ID scheme planned in #4109.
- Reuse `cmux diff` (#7101/#7102 context) for file diffs instead of building a second diff renderer.
- Sidebar git status counts (#959) should derive from the same data source so the numbers always agree.
- Works in cmux clone workspaces and in git worktrees; degrades gracefully (hidden or empty state) for non-git cwds.
- Localize all user-facing strings (English and Japanese).

### Out of scope (follow-ups)

- Multi-repo aggregation across workspaces.
- GitHub PR review comments inline (that stays in the browser pane / #609 inline review panel).

### Related

- #2526 (built-in way to view git changes; this issue is the concrete right-sidebar shape for it)
- #609 (inline diff / code review panel for agent-generated changes)
- #959 (sidebar git status counts)
- #3808 / #4109 (right-sidebar CLI verbs and action IDs)
- #7101 / #7102 (cmux diff viewer)
- #4705 (git metadata probing performance constraints)

title: Merge Files and Find into a single unified right-sidebar pane (one tool, not two vertical tabs)
author: austinywang
body:
### Problem or motivation

Files and Find live as two separate vertical tabs in the right sidebar, but they are really two views of the same thing: the project's file tree. Splitting them forces a mode switch for what should be one continuous flow (browse, then narrow down, then jump to a match), doubles the vertical-tab chrome, and makes the sidebar feel heavier than it needs to be.

They should be combined into one pane. Not two tabs, one surface.

### Proposed solution

Merge Files and Find into a single unified pane. The implementer should own the final design; the bar is that it looks and feels like one coherent tool. A recommended direction:

- One pane with a persistent search field at the top (the Find input) above the file tree.
- Empty query: the pane is the file tree, exactly like Files today.
- Typing a query: the tree gives way to results inline in the same pane (filename matches and content matches, grouped per file with match previews), with the same row styling as the tree so it reads as filtering the tree rather than switching tools.
- Clearing the query (Esc or the clear button) returns to the tree, preserving prior scroll/expansion state.
- Existing shortcuts keep working and land in the same pane: the Files shortcut focuses the tree, the Find shortcut focuses the search field. Both remain customizable through KeyboardShortcutSettings.
- The `cmux right-sidebar files` and `cmux right-sidebar find` CLI verbs (#3808) keep working: both open the unified pane, with `find` focusing the search field. Same for the planned action IDs in #4109.
- Vertical tab strip shows one icon where there were two.

Alternative shapes worth considering if they look better in practice: a segmented control inside one pane, or Xcode-style filter field at the bottom of the tree. Pick whatever reads best; the hard requirement is a single pane, not two vertical tabs.

### Notes

- Search state and tree state should not destroy each other when toggling.
- Localize all new user-facing strings (English and Japanese).
- Mind #7090 (Files/Find freeze at 100% CPU on launch) while touching this area; do not regress the lazy-loading behavior there.

### Related

- #3552 (right sidebar shortcut), #3808 (right-sidebar CLI), #4109 (right-sidebar action IDs), #7998 (title-bar toggle icon)

title: Settings window chrome regressed: bare legacy titlebar, no unified toolbar/sidebar toggle (AppKit-owned window, #7783)
author: austinywang
body:
## What

The Settings window renders with bare legacy "titled window" chrome: a thin opaque titlebar with a static, left-aligned "Settings" label next to the traffic lights, and no unified toolbar. The sidebar toggle button is missing, the sidebar and its search field no longer extend into the titlebar region, and the content scroll view starts below a plain title strip. It looks like a pre-Big Sur document window instead of the modern macOS Settings chrome (unified compact toolbar, transparent titlebar, full-size content, per-section title).

Observed on a current dev/nightly build (screenshot available; Account pane shown, same on every pane).

## Repro

1. Open cmux.
2. Press Cmd+, (or cmux > Settings) to open the Settings window.
3. Look at the titlebar.

Expected: modern Settings chrome — unified compact toolbar, sidebar toggle, transparent/tall titlebar with the `NavigationSplitView` sidebar and search integrated, section title over the detail column.

Actual: thin opaque legacy titlebar, static left-aligned "Settings", no toolbar buttons at all.

## Where

Since #7783 (follow-up #7800) the Settings window is AppKit-owned and built in `Sources/App/SettingsWindowFactory.swift`:

- `window.styleMask = [.titled, .closable, .miniaturizable, .resizable]` with no `fullSizeContentView`, and the factory never sets `toolbarStyle`, `titlebarAppearsTransparent`, or `titleVisibility`. `git grep` confirms none of these appear anywhere in the Settings window path.
- The factory relies on `hostingController.sceneBridgingOptions = [.toolbars, .title]` to bridge the SwiftUI `NavigationSplitView` toolbar (sidebar toggle, `.searchable(placement: .sidebar)`, `navigationTitle` from `Packages/macOS/CmuxSettingsUI/Sources/CmuxSettingsUI/Scene/SettingsWindowScene.swift`) into the AppKit titlebar. Per the screenshot, that bridge is not producing a toolbar, so the window falls back to the bare titled look.

## Related

- #5071 reported the same legacy-chrome symptom back when Settings was a SwiftUI `Window` scene (regression from #4975). It was never fixed; the window lifecycle then moved to AppKit ownership in #7783, which reproduces the same class of problem in the new code path. A proper fix in `SettingsWindowFactory` should close both.
- #7783 Make Settings open structurally reliable: AppKit-owned window lifecycle
- #7800 Settings follow-up: address post-merge review findings from #7783

title: Publish downloadable dSYM archives with NIGHTLY builds
author: austinywang
body:
## Summary

Follow-up from #8004: publish the dSYMs produced by the universal NIGHTLY Release build as a downloadable artifact tied to the exact build/SHA.

The NIGHTLY workflow already builds Release with `DEBUG_INFORMATION_FORMAT = dwarf-with-dsym` and uploads `build-universal/Build/Products/Release/` debug files to Sentry. However, the branch artifact and rolling `nightly` GitHub release currently publish DMGs, appcasts, and remote-daemon assets only. Maintainers therefore cannot retrieve the matching dSYM bundle for an on-device `sample`/`spindump` captured from a shipped NIGHTLY build.

## Proposed scope

- Archive the cmux app dSYM (and other cmux-owned dSYMs needed to resolve shipped frames) after the universal build, before cleanup.
- Name the archive with the immutable NIGHTLY build identifier and commit SHA.
- Upload it for branch NIGHTLY workflow runs and attach it to the rolling `nightly` release.
- Preserve the existing Sentry upload.
- Add a workflow guard proving the archive exists, contains the expected DWARF UUIDs, and matches the shipped app binary.
- Document retention/access expectations; dSYM publication must not include secrets or unrelated build products.

## Acceptance criteria

- A maintainer can download the exact dSYM archive for a published NIGHTLY build.
- `dwarfdump --uuid` for the shipped app binary and archived cmux dSYM match for both arm64 and x86_64.
- The archive is present in branch workflow artifacts and the rolling Nightly release assets.
- Sentry symbol upload continues to succeed.

This is intentionally separate from #8004 because it changes release artifact policy rather than sidebar interaction/runtime behavior.

title: iOS: constant "Connection lost - Retry" / all workspaces Disconnected while the Tailscale path is healthy (typing a few chars kills the session)
author: austinywang
body:
### Problem or motivation

The iOS app constantly drops its live connection to the paired Mac even when the underlying Tailscale link is healthy. This is now hitting external beta users, not just us.

Beta user report (email, 2026-07-12):

> I'm having lots of issues maintaining connection via tailscale between my mac and phone when using the iOS app. I get a "Connect lost - retry" popup consistently after entering just a couple characters into the terminal line in any of my Cmux workstations (see screenshot). Is this an issue that is common in the current beta?

Our own dogfooding shows the same symptoms:

- "Connection lost / Retry" pill appears over the terminal right after typing a few characters (reporter was on LTE; also reproduces on Wi-Fi).
- The Computers list shows "Disconnected. The live connection dropped. The selected cmux build may still be online. Tap Reconnect." with every workspace row marked Disconnected.
- A "Reconnecting..." overlay sits on top of live terminal content for long stretches; input gets queued ("Queued follow-up inputs") instead of delivered.
- At the same moment, the Tailscale app pings the same Mac with a direct connection at ~63 ms, so the tailnet path itself is fine; it is the cmux session layer that gives up.

Two screen recordings of the behavior exist (2026-07-10 12:29 PT, 2026-07-11 02:47 PT) and can be provided on request.

### Repro steps

1. Pair an iPhone with a Mac (standard Tailscale pairing).
2. Open any workspace with a terminal in the iOS app.
3. Type a couple of characters into the terminal.
4. Observe the "Connection lost - Retry" popup; the Computers list then shows everything Disconnected until a manual Reconnect.

### Expected

The mobile session layer should ride out transient stalls, roaming (Wi-Fi to cellular), and brief backgrounding without declaring the connection dead, should auto-reconnect without a manual tap, and should never let a healthy direct tailnet path surface as a constant "Connection lost". Durable, minimally-throwing connectivity is the top mobile priority right now.

### Likely area

- `Packages/iOS/CmuxMobileTransport` (`CmxNetworkByteTransport`, NWConnection viability/timeout handling)
- `Packages/iOS/CmuxMobileRPC` (`MobileCoreRPCSession` keepalive, write acks, reconnect policy)
- `Sources/Mobile/MobileHostService.swift` (host-side idle/ticket expiry, per-connection teardown)
- Reconnect UX: banner state machine and auto-retry backoff

### Related

- #7230 (trusted manual-host pairing for subnet-router setups)
- #6700 (pairing to a cloud VM, removing the Mac + Tailscale requirement)
- #8000 / #8001 (cmux hive umbrella; multi-device); the planned iroh migration does not replace fixing session durability on the current Tailscale TCP path.

title: NIGHTLY sidebar livelock persists after #7954: per-row AppKit hover bridge reenters SwiftUI updates
author: lawrencecchen
body:
## Summary

cmux NIGHTLY has hung twice on July 13, 2026, including after updating to a build containing #7954. Both incidents peg the main thread near 100% CPU inside a non-returning SwiftUI transaction involving `LazyVStack` placement and `NSViewRepresentable` platform-view updates.

The updated-build capture plus unified logs point to a lifecycle feedback loop introduced by #7545: every mounted workspace row contains an AppKit hover reconciler whose `updateTrackingAreas` / `viewDidMoveToWindow` lifecycle callbacks synchronously mutate SwiftUI row state. SwiftUI logs state-during-update and reentrant-layout faults while the sampled main thread remains inside `GraphHost.flushTransactions()`.

Related umbrella incidents: https://github.com/manaflow-ai/cmux/issues/2586 and https://github.com/manaflow-ai/cmux/issues/7136.

## Incident A: pre-update NIGHTLY

- Version: `0.64.17-nightly.2921385573701`
- Commit: `c85ed9b8f5`
- PID: `19435`
- CPU: approximately 100%
- Physical footprint: approximately 2.8 GB
- Unresponsive for: 405 seconds
- Local capture: `/tmp/cmux-hang-20260713-151246`
- Main-thread stack remained in `NSHostingView.beginTransaction -> GraphHost.flushTransactions -> LazySubviewPlacements -> LazyStack -> ForEach`.
- The same sample also contains `PlatformViewChild.updateValue -> NSView.adoptEnvironment` work.

The process was terminated only after evidence capture and then relaunched for the update.

## Incident B: updated NIGHTLY containing #7954

- Version: `0.64.17-nightly.2928716483201`
- Commit: `a023bef5e5`
- Workflow run: `29287164832`
- PID: `9255`
- Uptime at capture: 24 minutes
- CPU: 99.7%
- RSS: 1.1 GB
- Physical footprint: 1.75 GB
- Threads: 114 at fast capture, 133 during spindump
- Spindump: unresponsive for 125 seconds before sampling
- Local capture: `/tmp/cmux-hang-20260713-154653`

Footprint highlights:

- 929 MB `MALLOC_SMALL`
- 350 MB `IOSurface`
- 262 MB unmapped graphics footprint
- 98 MB `IOAccelerator`

All 181 spindump samples of the main thread remained in one `NSHostingView.beginTransaction -> GraphHost.flushTransactions` transaction. The fast sample contained:

```text
7359 flushObservers
7346 GraphHost.flushTransactions
6353 GraphHost.runTransaction
5390 AttributeGraph Subgraph.update
4509 AttributeGraph UpdateStack.update
689  LazySubviewPlacements.updateValue
654  LazySubviewPlacements.placeSubviews
600  LazyStack.place
534  ForEachState.forEachItem
414  PlatformViewChild.updateValue
352  AppKitPlatformViewHost.coreUpdateEnvironment
233  NSView.adoptEnvironment
127  NSAppearance.appearance(from:allowsVibrantBlending:)
85   NSCompositeAppearance.appearanceByApplyingTintColor
```

The process unified log from launch through capture contains:

- 18 `Publishing changes from within view updates is not allowed`
- 120 `Modifying state during view update, this will cause undefined behavior`
- 648 `NSHostingView is being laid out reentrantly while rendering its SwiftUI content`

Immediately around the final transition into the sustained hang:

```text
15:45:03.984 Publishing changes from within view updates is not allowed
15:45:04.001 Modifying state during view update, this will cause undefined behavior
15:45:04.023 NSHostingView is being laid out reentrantly while rendering its SwiftUI content
```

The process remains alive at the time this issue was filed so further debugger attachment remains possible.

## Suspected direct lifecycle loop

#7545 replaced row `.onHover` handling with `SidebarWorkspaceRowHoverReconciler`, an `NSViewRepresentable` mounted in every workspace row and group header.

`SidebarWorkspaceRowHoverReconcilerView.updateTrackingAreas()` and `viewDidMoveToWindow()` call `reconcileCurrentPointerLocation()`. That calls `onPointerHoverChanged`, whose SwiftUI closure mutates the row's `@State` interaction model. Those AppKit methods can run while SwiftUI is updating or adopting the representable's environment, producing the exact state-during-update and reentrant-layout faults in the system log.

Every workspace row also contains `MiddleClickCapture`, another `NSViewRepresentable`, so the lazy graph has at least two platform views per mounted row before inline rename or context-menu tracking appears.

The #7545 regression test directly instantiates `SidebarWorkspaceRowHoverReconcilerView` and invokes `reconcilePointerLocation`. It does not mount the representable inside a visible `NSHostingView` or `LazyVStack`, so it cannot exercise or detect SwiftUI update reentrancy.

Confidence is high that this is the remaining structural trigger. Release symbols are stripped, so the sample cannot name the exact cmux representable type. The stack topology, runtime faults, codepath, and July 7 introduction align. The fix should still verify this hypothesis with a visible-window red test before changing production code.

## Why earlier fixes did not close the class

- #2328 and #2601 fixed specific early sidebar layout loops.
- #4736 restored lazy rendering and isolated drag state.
- #5859 removed notification-store invalidation pressure.
- #6033, #6188, and #6870 removed whole-list measurement and guarded against custom layouts.
- #7221 added a 300-row behavioral scale gate, but its synthetic host does not exercise a visible/key window, AppKit environment adoption, appearance/tint propagation, or lifecycle callbacks from per-row representables.
- #7527 reduced geometry churn.
- #7754 reduced sustained process-title invalidation and memoized row snapshots.
- #7954 pruned terminated observation streams. Incident B occurred in a build containing it after only 24 minutes, ruling out long-uptime observer accumulation as the complete cause.

These changes removed amplifiers. None established the invariant that lazy sidebar rows cannot synchronously publish SwiftUI state from AppKit lifecycle callbacks.

## Proposed work

### Immediate containment

1. Add a visible-window regression test first that reproduces the current lifecycle path and fails on the existing implementation.
2. Remove the #7545 per-row AppKit hover reconciler and restore native SwiftUI hover handling temporarily.
3. Remove or relocate `MiddleClickCapture` so workspace rows contain no `NSViewRepresentable` children.

### Durable architecture

1. Give sidebar interaction lifecycle one `@MainActor` owner outside the lazy row graph.
2. Feed rows immutable interaction snapshots such as hovered row ID, rather than letting native lifecycle callbacks mutate row-owned state.
3. Make hover, middle-click, drag, and context-menu changes originate only from user input events, never `makeNSView`, `updateNSView`, `layout`, `updateTrackingAreas`, or `viewDidMoveToWindow`.
4. Add a source guard that rejects `NSViewRepresentable` beneath workspace and group-header rows.
5. Move virtualization to `NSCollectionView` with a diffable immutable row snapshot if the SwiftUI lazy-list boundary continues to admit layout livelocks.

## Acceptance criteria

- Regression test uses a visible/key `NSWindow` and the production sidebar hierarchy.
- Exercise 300 mixed-height workspaces, scrolling, appearance/accent changes, title/unread/metadata churn, row remounts, context menus, stationary-pointer hover, and drag state.
- Zero `Publishing changes from within view updates` faults.
- Zero `Modifying state during view update` faults.
- Zero reentrant `NSHostingView` layout faults.
- Main-thread heartbeat and tagged debug socket remain responsive throughout accelerated churn.
- Row realization remains bounded to the visible viewport.
- Static guard prevents future per-row AppKit representables.
- NIGHTLY publishes dSYMs so future captures resolve cmux-owned frames.

## Evidence preservation

Local artifacts on the reporting Mac:

```text
/tmp/cmux-hang-20260713-151246
/tmp/cmux-hang-20260713-154653
```

Each directory includes `meta.txt`, `sample.txt`, `spindump.txt`, `footprint.txt`, `vmmap-summary.txt`, process listings, and capture logs.

title: Feature: first-class BYO VPS backend with fully direct data path (no cloud proxy for PTY/browser/agent traffic)
author: austinywang
body:
## Summary

Make a user-owned VPS a first-class cmux backend with a fully direct data path: no manaflow cloud proxy in the PTY, browser, or agent traffic. The goal is Cloud-VM-grade UX (one-click provision, resilient sessions, multi-client access including iOS) on the SSH-direct transport, so users stop having to choose between polish (Cloud VM through the cloud proxy) and sovereignty (manual SSH workspace).

## Background: the two remote paths today

**Cloud VM workspaces** route through the manaflow control plane: the Vercel app (`web/app/api/vm/**`, `web/services/vms/**`, Postgres `cloud_vms` / `cloud_vm_leases`) performs the REST handshake, then PTY and browser data flow over WebSocket proxies to provider VM endpoints. The client never dials the VM directly. This is what buys one-click provisioning and works-from-anywhere (both ends dial out, so NAT never matters), but every keystroke, agent transcript byte, and browser frame transits cloud infrastructure.

**SSH workspaces** are already proxyless: the Mac dials the host directly, `cmuxd-remote --persistent-server` owns PTYs on the box, browser panes tunnel SOCKS5/CONNECT over the daemon stream RPC via the local proxy broker, and a reverse-forwarded TCP relay (HMAC challenge-response auth, per-session `CMUX_SOCKET_PATH=127.0.0.1:<relay_port>`) lets remote `cmux` CLI calls reach the Mac (`docs/remote-daemon-spec.md`). No manaflow servers touch the data path.

The feature is: close the gap between these two so "point cmux at `root@my-vps`" yields the Cloud VM experience with the SSH path's privacy properties.

## Motivation

- **Privacy / enterprise trust**: terminal I/O, agent transcripts, and browser traffic never leave user infrastructure. This is a hard requirement for many companies evaluating cmux.
- **Latency**: one hop (client to VPS) instead of client to cloud proxy to VM.
- **Cost & scale**: manaflow stops paying egress/proxy capacity for traffic that can be peer-to-peer; users bring cheap VPSes instead of metered Cloud VMs.
- **Resilience / lock-in**: workspaces keep working if cmux.com is unreachable; "your infra, your data" is a clean answer to vendor lock-in concerns.
- A VPS is exactly the case where a rendezvous proxy is least needed: the backend has a public address. The proxy earns its keep when NEITHER end is dialable, which is not this case.

## Proposal (phased)

### Phase 1 — SSH-path parity (prerequisites, mostly existing issues)

Direct VPS is only credible if the SSH path is not second-class. Blockers, tracked separately:

- #7978 — panes die permanently after Mac sleep; persistent PTYs survive but never reattach; remote agents get no `resume_binding` (agent actively working on this; do not duplicate).
- #3930 — surface the SSH reconnect lifecycle in UI.
- #6549 — reconnect budget exhausted while 1Password SSH agent is locked on wake.
- #2673 — first-class remote shell/tmux support (related UX context).

This issue does not re-implement those; it depends on them.

### Phase 2 — One-click VPS onboarding (core of this issue)

`cmux` should turn a bare `user@host` + key into a fully provisioned backend in under a minute:

- **Bootstrap over SSH**: a guided flow (CLI: `cmux vps add user@host`; and Settings UI) that detects arch/distro, uploads or downloads the matching `cmuxd-remote` binary, verifies checksum, installs a systemd (or OpenRC) unit running `cmuxd-remote --persistent-server`, and starts it. Idempotent: re-running upgrades in place and never destroys live sessions (drain/handoff or restart-with-reattach, leveraging the persistent-session model).
- **Version management**: daemon reports its version in the handshake; client offers one-click upgrade when incompatible; N-1 compatibility window so a Mac update does not brick remote sessions.
- **Health surface**: sidebar shows daemon state (running/degraded/needs-upgrade), PTY session count, and last-seen, driven by real daemon signals, not inferred from stale connection state (cf. #7828's class of bug).
- **Teardown**: `cmux vps remove` cleanly stops the unit and optionally preserves session state.

### Phase 3 — Multi-client direct access (iOS without the proxy)

- iOS speaks the same daemon protocol directly to the VPS (public IP or user-owned overlay like Tailscale/WireGuard as plain transport). The cloud may assist with discovery and push notifications only; PTY/browser bytes never transit it.
- **Pairing/auth without the control plane**: generalize the relay's HMAC token model. Pairing mints per-client credentials on the VPS (Mac generates, displays QR; phone scans; credential is a client-scoped key registered with the daemon). Mutual auth on every connection; revocation list on the daemon.
- Session continuity: the persistent server already decouples PTY lifetime from attachments, so Mac and iPhone attach to the same sessions concurrently or in turn.

### Non-goals

- NAT traversal for backends without a public address (that remains the cloud proxy's job; do not build hole-punching here).
- Replacing managed Cloud VMs. The product line is: cloud proxy stays the default for managed VMs and NAT-locked cases; direct is guaranteed whenever the backend is publicly dialable; the UI states which mode is active.
- Billing/metering of BYO VPS resources.

## Design constraints and open questions

1. **Security defaults**: bootstrap must not weaken the host (no new open ports by default; everything rides the SSH transport as today; daemon listens on loopback/UDS unless multi-client direct is enabled, at which point it binds a mutual-auth TLS port explicitly).
2. **Trust bootstrap for iOS**: QR-based credential handoff vs. cloud-assisted discovery-only exchange; decide default and document threat model.
3. **Upgrade safety**: daemon upgrade with live PTYs must reattach cleanly (depends on the #7978 reattach machinery being solid).
4. **Distro matrix**: define the supported set for v1 (systemd x86_64/arm64 Debian/Ubuntu/Fedora; report-only elsewhere) and fail with actionable messages off-matrix.
5. **Mode visibility**: workspace badge/inspector should show "Direct (no cloud proxy)" vs "Cloud-proxied" so the privacy property is verifiable by the user, plus a doc page stating exactly what does and does not touch manaflow servers in each mode.

## Acceptance criteria

- From a fresh Debian 12 VPS with only an SSH key: `cmux vps add user@host` (or the Settings flow) produces a workspace with persistent PTYs, working browser panes, working agent hooks/notifications, and daemon auto-start on reboot, with zero manual steps on the host.
- Wireshark/`lsof` on the client shows no manaflow-owned endpoints in the PTY/browser data path for a direct-mode workspace.
- Killing cmux.com connectivity (block the domain) leaves a direct-mode workspace fully functional (minus optional discovery/push).
- Daemon upgrade via the client preserves running sessions and reattaches all panes.
- UI clearly labels direct vs proxied mode.

## Rollout sketch

Phase 2 can land behind a settings flag (`Experimental: BYO VPS onboarding`) and dogfood on team VPSes; Phase 3 gates on the pairing design review. Each phase splits into normal-sized PRs (bootstrap CLI, daemon unit management, health surface, upgrade path, then iOS transport + pairing).

title: hive M2: designate a master (source of truth) per runtime; other instances become configurable views
author: austinywang
body:
Part of #8000 (cmux hive umbrella). Milestone 2, builds on #8001 (multi-device on macOS).

### Problem or motivation

Once multiple devices can attach to the same runtime, users need to designate which instance is the source of truth (the master) and make the others explicit views. Today every attached device is an equal last-writer-wins controller (v1 model from #8001).

### Scope

1. **Designation model**: per-runtime role state: exactly one master (the instance that owns execution), any number of views. Persisted in the hive registry (extend the `/api/devices` device records or a sibling table) and reflected live to all attached clients.
2. **Designation UX**: from any attached device, promote/demote with confirmation on the current master (Settings > Computers and a workspace-level affordance). Handle master-offline: views stay attached read-only or with control, per config, and can claim mastership per policy.
3. **Per-device control config**: make it super configurable: per device (and optionally per workspace) choose full control, input allowed with arbitration, or read-only view. Defaults preserve v1 behavior (full control for all).
4. **Input arbitration**: when a non-master sends input in arbitrated mode, master gets a lightweight grant/deny surface; grants can be remembered per device.
5. **State signaling**: views clearly render their role (view-only badge, focus behavior, blocked-input feedback).

### Out of scope

- Cloud/`cmuxd-remote` runtimes (M3), iroh transport (M4), runtime-authoritative app state (M5).

### Acceptance criteria

- Two Macs + an iPhone attached to one runtime; exactly one shows master, others show their configured role.
- Demoting/promoting takes effect on all attached clients within seconds without reconnecting.
- Read-only view cannot inject input at the protocol level (server-enforced, not just UI-disabled).
- Role config survives app restarts and registry round-trips.

title: hive M1: add multiple devices on macOS and view/control another Mac's workspaces (Tailscale)
author: austinywang
body:
Part of #8000 (cmux hive umbrella). Milestone 1.

### Problem or motivation

The iOS app can already add multiple devices to an account and control paired Macs. The macOS app cannot: it can only be a host. Bring the same capability to macOS so a Mac can list the other Macs on the account, pair with them, and open their workspaces as live views with full control, exactly like the phone does today.

### Scope

1. **Settings > Computers on macOS**: list the account's registered devices with presence, reusing `GET /api/devices` plus the local-first paired-device store. Allow unpairing.
2. **Mac-to-Mac pairing**: pair another Mac on the same account. Reuse the existing pairing payload/URL-scheme model (`CmxPairingURLScheme`, `CmxManualPairingEntry`); a Mac has no camera, so support showing a QR/deep link on the host and manual code / deep-link entry on the client.
3. **macOS viewer client**: connect to a paired Mac's `MobileHostService` over Tailscale TCP and render its workspaces inside the local app: workspace list, attached terminal view (render grid stream), keyboard input. Full control, last-writer-wins; no role system yet (that is M2, the umbrella has details).
4. **Package retargeting**: the client stack lives under `Packages/iOS/` (`CmuxMobileRPC`, `CmuxMobileTransport`, `CmuxMobilePairedMac`). Add macOS platform support or hoist shared pieces to `Packages/Shared/` so the macOS app can consume them. Keep the iOS app building unchanged.

### Out of scope (later milestones)

- Master/view designation, roles, input arbitration (M2).
- Cloud/`cmuxd-remote` persistent sessions (M3).
- iroh relay client transport; Tailscale TCP only for now (M4).
- Browser panes / non-terminal surfaces in the remote view.

### Acceptance criteria

- From Mac A, Settings > Computers shows Mac B (paired, presence indicator).
- Opening Mac B from Mac A shows Mac B's workspace list and a live terminal view; typing on Mac A reaches Mac B's PTY.
- Disconnect/reconnect (network blip, Mac B app restart) recovers without relaunching Mac A.
- iOS app behavior and pairing are unchanged.

### Key reuse map

`Sources/Mobile/MobileHostService.swift` (host side, already serves everything needed), `Packages/iOS/CmuxMobileRPC` (`MobileCoreRPCClient`, render grid events), `Packages/iOS/CmuxMobileTransport` (`CmxNetworkByteTransport`, Tailscale), `Packages/iOS/CmuxMobilePairedMac` (paired store), `Packages/Shared/CMUXMobileCore` (pairing, transport wire model), `Sources/Cloud/DeviceRegistryClient.swift` (device self-registration), `web/app/api/devices/route.ts` (registry API).

title: cmux hive: multi-device runtimes with master/view roles (umbrella)
author: austinywang
body:
### Vision

cmux hive: sessions (terminals, agents, workspace layout) live in a runtime, and every device is just a view. A session started on the desktop can be picked up from an iPhone, a web client, or another Mac mid-conversation. Any cmux instance can be designated the master (source of truth) while others attach as views. Runtimes can be a Mac app, another Mac on the account, or a cloud VM. This is the "tmux for agents" direction and it subsumes multiple existing requests (multiple devices, VPS support, session history, remote reattach).

### Concepts

- **Runtime**: owns live sessions (PTYs, agents, layout, scrollback). Kinds: local Mac app (today's behavior), another Mac acting as host, cloud runtime (`cmuxd-remote` on a VM).
- **View**: any client attached to a runtime (Mac app, iOS app, web). Same workspace UI whether the runtime is local or remote.
- **Hive registry**: account-level list of runtimes and devices with presence and roles, extending the existing `/api/devices` registry that iOS already uses.
- **Roles**: a designated master per runtime plus attached views. v1 control model: full control for all attached devices, last-writer-wins (tmux mental model). Role/arbitration configurability comes later.
- **Transports**: priority-ordered loopback, then Tailscale TCP (works today), then iroh relay (token endpoint shipped in #7879; client transport not yet built).

### Existing groundwork (verified in-repo)

- `daemon/remote/` (`cmuxd-remote`, Go): persistent PTY sessions that survive surface close, reconnect, and app relaunch; slot files + auth tokens; WS transport with single-use leases. Cloud VM stack (`web/services/vms/`) already boots it. Spec: `docs/remote-daemon-spec.md`.
- `cmux-tui/` (Rust): headless multiplexer, one-runtime-many-views (terminals, browser panes, layout tree, agent state) over Unix socket + WebSocket (protocol v6), with an xterm.js React web frontend.
- Multi-device registry + pairing: `web/app/api/devices/route.ts` (Postgres + Stack auth, team-scoped), QR/deep-link pairing (`Packages/Shared/CMUXMobileCore`), local-first paired-Mac store (`Packages/iOS/CmuxMobilePairedMac`, `Packages/Shared/CmuxSyncStore`).
- Mac-as-host: `Sources/Mobile/MobileHostService.swift` already serves terminal render grids, workspace lists, notifications, and agent chat to phones over authenticated Tailscale TCP (`Packages/iOS/CmuxMobileTransport`, `Packages/iOS/CmuxMobileRPC`).
- iroh relay: `web/app/api/relay/token/route.ts` live with a 7-region fleet; wire types already declare `.iroh` / `.websocket` transport kinds; client side is experiment-only.
- Local session snapshots v1 (`Sources/SessionPersistence.swift` and friends): layout, capped scrollback, agent sessions across relaunch, local files only.

### What is missing (net-new)

- Master/view designation and input arbitration (no concept exists anywhere).
- Mac-to-Mac attach (registry and host stack assume Mac-host to phone-viewer).
- Shipping client-side iroh transport.
- Server-authoritative session state (the daemon holds PTYs but not workspace/agent/browser state).

### Milestones

- [ ] M1: add multiple devices on macOS; view and control another Mac's workspaces over Tailscale (see milestone issue)
- [ ] M2: designate a runtime as source of truth; other instances become views (see milestone issue)
- [ ] M3: persistent remote session backed by `cmuxd-remote`: Mac + iPhone both attach with full scrollback and agent state
- [ ] M4: iroh client transport so hive works without Tailscale
- [ ] M5: runtime-authoritative state: the Mac app itself reattaches to its own sessions after relaunch

### Decisions so far

- Multi-device (Mac-to-Mac) lands first, then master/view designation, then the cloud persistent runtime.
- v1 control model: full control for all attached devices, last-writer-wins. Configurable roles arrive with M2.
- Transport: Tailscale first; iroh client transport is its own milestone (M4).

### Related issues this subsumes or advances

#1037 (remote access), #2587 (remote reattach from phone), #2823 (sessions lost after restart), #3342 (agent session recovery), #2696 (persistent SSH relay daemon), #1664 (first-class ssh workspaces), #5454 / #7978 (SSH session persistence), #5497 (render-suspend tier), #7879 (relay token endpoint, merged), #2558 (CLI primitives over ssh).

title: Add top-right title-bar icon to toggle the right sidebar
author: austinywang
body:
### Problem or motivation

The left sidebar has a Show/Hide toggle icon in the title bar, but the right sidebar has no clickable UI affordance at all. Today the only ways to toggle it are the Cmd+Opt+B shortcut (#3552) and the `cmux right-sidebar` CLI (#3808). Users who never learn the shortcut may never discover the right sidebar's Files/Find/Vault/Sessions/Feed/Dock views.

### Proposed solution

Add a built-in toggle icon at the top right of the window (title bar, right side) that shows/hides the right sidebar, mirroring the existing left-sidebar toggle icon on the left.

Details:

- Icon placement: far right of the title bar, symmetric with the left sidebar toggle.
- Use a right-sidebar SF Symbol (e.g. `sidebar.right` / `sidebar.trailing`) so the affordance reads correctly.
- Clicking toggles visibility of the right sidebar; state should reflect current visibility (filled/highlighted when open, matching the left toggle's behavior).
- Tooltip with the action name and the Cmd+Opt+B shortcut.
- Localize the tooltip/accessibility label (English and Japanese).

### Related

- #3552 added the keyboard shortcut (Cmd+Opt+B).
- #3808 added the CLI surface.
- #4109 proposes exposing right-sidebar actions as built-in action IDs for the configurable surface tab bar / title bar, and mentions a title-bar icon as a bonus. This issue is the concrete ask for that built-in, on-by-default top-right toggle icon; the action-ID work in #4109 could be the underlying mechanism but should not block shipping the default icon.

### Alternatives considered

- Config-only button via #4109 action IDs: works for power users, but discoverability requires an on-by-default icon like the left sidebar has.

title: ssh-tmux fails and strands an empty window when the destination's mirror was moved to a shared window
author: nathang21
body:
## Summary

If a remote-tmux mirror workspace has been moved out of its dedicated window (e.g. via `cmux move-workspace-to-window` into a window shared with other workspaces), a subsequent `cmux ssh-tmux <same-destination>` does not adopt the existing live mirror. It fails with `Error: host unreachable: could not mirror any tmux session on <destination>` — while the host is reachable and the existing mirror keeps working — and strands a newly created empty window, which cmux then populates with a blank local "Terminal" workspace.

## Environment

- cmux 0.64.17 (97) [9ed29d81a], macOS 26.5.1 (Apple Silicon), Remote tmux beta enabled
- Reproduced with destinations over both plain OpenSSH and Tailscale SSH

## Repro

1. `cmux ssh-tmux <dest>` → mirror workspace in its own window. (At this point, re-running `cmux ssh-tmux <dest>` correctly reuses it and prints `OK host=... window=...`.)
2. `cmux move-workspace-to-window --workspace <mirror-ws> --window <some-shared-window>` — mirror keeps working fine in the shared window.
3. `cmux ssh-tmux <dest>` again.
4. Output: `Connecting to <dest>… Error: host unreachable: could not mirror any tmux session on <dest>` — and a new empty window is left open (auto-filled with a blank local Terminal workspace).

## Expected

Either adopt/reuse the existing mirror workspace regardless of which window it currently sits in (matching the reuse behavior from step 1), or at minimum fail without leaving a stray window behind. The "host unreachable" message is also misleading for this failure mode.

(Related: #7264 changes the default placement to the current window — that would make "mirror in a shared window" the normal state, so the reuse path probably needs to handle it either way.)

title: list-workspaces reports "id": null for remote-tmux mirror workspaces
author: nathang21
body:
## Summary

`cmux list-workspaces --window <id> --json` reports `"id": null` for remote-tmux mirror workspaces (Remote tmux beta), even though the workspace demonstrably has a UUID — `cmux list-windows --json` returns that same workspace's UUID as the window's `selected_workspace_id`.

## Environment

- cmux 0.64.17 (97) [9ed29d81a], macOS 26.5.1 (Apple Silicon), Remote tmux beta enabled

## Repro

1. `cmux ssh-tmux <host>` → mirror workspace appears in its dedicated window.
2. `cmux list-windows --json` → the mirror window entry shows a real UUID in `selected_workspace_id`.
3. `cmux list-workspaces --window <that-window> --json` → the mirror workspace object has `"id": null` (while `ref`, `title`, `has_custom_title` etc. are all populated).

Local (non-mirror) workspaces return proper UUIDs from the same command.

## Impact

Scripting against mirrors can't obtain a stable workspace handle from `list-workspaces` — `id` is the documented stable identifier, and refs (`workspace:N`) renumber on reorder/move. Workarounds: read the UUID from `list-windows.selected_workspace_id` (only works while the mirror is its window's selected workspace) or use refs immediately after listing.

Notably the null seems persistent (not just a startup race): it reproduces minutes after the mirror is attached and interactive.

title: remote-tmux: mirror shows duplicated input echo and drops prompt lines on attach — only over Tailscale SSH transport
author: nathang21
body:
## Summary

Remote-tmux mirrors (beta) show two rendering artifacts on one of my two mirrored hosts — and only on the host reached over **Tailscale SSH** (tailscaled's userspace SSH server). The same cmux build mirroring a host over plain OpenSSH renders cleanly. Server-side tmux content is verified correct in both cases, so this is display-only.

## Environment

- cmux 0.64.17 (97) [9ed29d81a], macOS 26.5.1 (Apple Silicon), Remote tmux beta enabled
- Host A: Ubuntu 26.04, tmux 3.6, reached via **Tailscale SSH** (no OpenSSH involved) → artifacts
- Host B: Synology DSM 7.3, tmux 3.6b, reached via **plain OpenSSH** (port 22222, ControlMaster) → renders correctly
- Both hosts: zsh + powerlevel10k with effectively identical configs (nerdfont-v3, `POWERLEVEL9K_TRANSIENT_PROMPT=always`, `POWERLEVEL9K_INSTANT_PROMPT=verbose`, same prompt elements; no zsh-autosuggestions / zsh-syntax-highlighting on either). Panes on both report `TERM=tmux-256color`.

## Artifact 1 — typed input echoed into the output line

Typing a command in a mirrored pane on Host A doubles the input into the next line, merged with the command's output:

```
❯ hostname
hostnamenuc-14-pro        <- expected just "nuc-14-pro"
```

## Artifact 2 — prompt lines/segments dropped on attach (seed)

On Host A, the seeded scrollback after attaching is missing most of the p10k prompt (dir/vcs segments absent, bare `❯` remains). Freshly drawn prompts after the attach render fully.

Server-side verification that tmux itself holds the complete prompt (raw escapes show the full dir segment + powerline glyphs that the mirror doesn't paint):

```
$ tmux capture-pane -p -e -t NUC:1 | tail -1
^[[38;5;254m^[[44m ^[[1m^[[38;5;255m~^[[0m... (full segments present)
```

## Why I suspect transport timing

The only variable that differs between the clean and the affected host is the SSH transport. Tailscale SSH delivers output with different latency/chunk boundaries than OpenSSH. Given `docs/remote-tmux-sizing-timers.md` documents the mirror pipeline's load-bearing timers (attach redraw kick ~350 ms vs tmux's ~250 ms resize coalescing), a timing-sensitive seed/render path + a lumpier transport on exactly one host would explain artifacts on exactly that host.

Possibly overlaps PR #7106 (mirror seed render fix); the input-echo duplication may or may not be covered by it.

## Repro

1. Enable Remote tmux beta; have a tmux session running on a host reachable only via Tailscale SSH.
2. `cmux ssh-tmux <tailscale-ssh-host>`
3. Observe missing prompt content in the seeded scrollback; type any command and observe the echoed input merged into the output line.
4. Repeat against an OpenSSH host — both artifacts absent.

Happy to provide more traces/logs.
